title: Anonymous usage and health metrics collection
why_it_matters: By default, Codex periodically sends a small amount of anonymous usage and health data back to OpenAI so the Codex team can see what features and configuration options are used and detect when Codex is not working correctly. OpenAI's documentation states these metrics do not contain personally identifiable information (PII) and that metrics collection is independent of OTel log/trace export, but it is still non-essential telemetry sent by default unless disabled, and this rule does not inspect every configuration layer that can override a user-level analytics.enabled value.

outcomes.message: The inspected user-level config disables anonymous usage and health metrics collection (analytics.enabled = false).

outcomes.when: analytics.enabled is explicitly true in the user-level config
outcomes.message: The inspected user-level config explicitly enables anonymous usage and health metrics collection (analytics.enabled = true), the same behavior Codex applies by default.

remediation.summary: Disable anonymous usage and health metrics collection.

outcomes.when: analytics.enabled is unset in the user-level config or the user-level config is absent
outcomes.message: Effective usage analytics telemetry posture cannot be determined from an unset user-level value because uninspected configuration layers may supply or override it.
outcomes.unknown_reason: analytics.enabled is unset in the user-level config; Codex's documented default sends anonymous usage and health metrics unless disabled, but uninspected system, profile, trusted-project, or CLI layers may determine the effective value.

outcomes.when: analytics.enabled is set to a value outside boolean true/false
outcomes.message: analytics.enabled is set to an unrecognized value — raw values are never displayed.
outcomes.unknown_reason: analytics.enabled is set to an unrecognized value — raw values are never displayed

sources.title: Codex configuration reference
sources.notes: Documents analytics.enabled as a boolean key: "Enable or disable analytics for this machine/profile. When unset, the client default applies." Re-verified 2026-07-17; page content unchanged since the 2026-07-16 retrieval cited by codex-history-persist-01 (identical normalized content hash).

sources.title: Codex advanced configuration
sources.notes: States: "By default, Codex periodically sends a small amount of anonymous usage and health data back to OpenAI... These metrics don't contain any personally identifiable information (PII). Metrics collection is independent of OTel log/trace export," and documents disabling metrics collection entirely by setting [analytics] enabled = false. Re-verified 2026-07-17; page content unchanged since the 2026-07-16 retrieval cited by codex-history-persist-01 (identical normalized content hash).

limitations: Only the user-level CODEX_HOME/config.toml is inspected; system config, selected profiles, trusted-project config, and CLI overrides are not inspected.
An explicit user-level value describes that layer only and does not prove the effective value for every profile, trusted project, or invocation.
This rule does not inspect the content of any individual emitted metric event beyond OpenAI's documented claim that metrics omit personally identifiable information (PII).
This rule does not inspect OTel log/trace export, which OpenAI's documentation describes as independent of analytics.enabled.
Auth method (ChatGPT sign-in vs API key) changes data-policy interpretation and is user-confirmed-or-unknown; this rule never infers it.
unknown_conditions: Config file is a symlink or non-regular file (not followed).
analytics.enabled is unset in the user-level config or the user-level config is absent; other configuration layers are not inspected.
analytics.enabled set to a value outside boolean true/false (raw values are never displayed).
Config file exceeds parse bounds (size > 1 MiB or nesting depth > 32).
